title: A Topological Soliton Model for Ball Lightning: Theory and Numerical Verification with the 3D Gross-Pitaevskii Equation
abstract: Ball lightning is one of the most mysterious atmospheric phenomena, whose long lifetime, penetrative ability, and stability are difficult to explain with traditional physical models. This paper proposes a novel theoretical framework, interpreting ball lightning as a projection of a high-dimensional topological soliton into three-dimensional space. Its essence is described by a nonlinear Schrödinger equation with attractive interaction, protected by a non-zero topological charge. Through numerical simulation of the three-dimensional Gross-Pitaevskii equation, we verify the core predictions of this model: in a Bose-Einstein condensate with attractive interactions, solitons carrying topological charge exhibit: (1)long-lived stability (topological charge conserved under perturbations); (2)low transmission probability (due to minimal overlap integral resulting from orthogonality with the ground state wavefunction); (3)energy and size scales consistent with actual observations. Theoretical analysis indicates that the soliton lifetime is governed by the system's decoherence rate, providing a natural explanation for the observed second-scale lifetimes. This work not only offers a self-consistent physical explanation for ball lightning but also provides a concrete scheme for the experimental preparation and observation of three-dimensional topological solitons.
summary: This paper proposes a new theoretical model for ball lightning, treating it as a 3D topological soliton described by the Gross-Pitaevskii equation. Numerical simulations verify that these solitons are stable, can pass through barriers via wavefunction orthogonality, and match observed energy and lifetime scales. The work provides a unified physical explanation for a long-standing atmospheric mystery using the framework of ultracold gas dynamics.
main_problem: Explaining the physical nature of ball lightning, specifically its long lifetime, stability, and ability to penetrate obstacles.
main_result: The paper proposes that ball lightning is a projection of a high-dimensional topological soliton, demonstrating through simulations that such structures possess the required stability, penetrability, and physical scales.
method: 3D numerical simulations of the Gross-Pitaevskii equation using a Fourier Spectral Method and a fourth-order Runge-Kutta algorithm.
model_or_system: A 3D Gross-Pitaevskii equation (GPE) with attractive interactions (g < 0), modeling the system as a Bose-Einstein condensate-like structure with a non-zero topological charge.
key_observables: Topological charge (Q), lifetime (tau), transmission coefficient (T), soliton width (sigma), and total energy (E).
important_parameters: Interaction strength (g approx -2.0), topological winding number (n=1), trap frequency (omega = 0.3), and dissipation coefficient (gamma = 0.02).
main_assumptions: The phenomenon can be modeled as a projection of a high-dimensional topological soliton into 3D space; the stability is governed by the decoherence rate.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows initial wavefunction density and phase winding; Figure 2 visualizes the 3D soliton isosurface; Figure 3 plots the time evolution of topological charge and energy.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the ball lightning problem, proposes a topological soliton model based on the GPE, details the numerical simulation methods, presents results regarding stability and penetrability, compares predictions to observations, and concludes by comparing the model to existing atmospheric physics theories.
why_interesting: It applies the physics of topological protection and Gross-Pitaevskii dynamics—central to BEC and many-body studies—to an atmospheric phenomenon, suggesting that 3D topological solitons can be experimentally prepared in BEC or nonlinear optical systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 2: 3D isosurface visualization of the topological soliton
Figure 3: Time evolution of topological charge and total energy
